items.question: You are given a feedforward network: input (20 features) → hidden (50, ReLU) → hidden (30, ReLU) → output (3). What is the depth and what are the hidden layer widths?
items.answer: Depth is 2 (two hidden layers). Hidden widths are 50 in the first hidden layer and 30 in the second. The output layer width is 3 (e.g. three-class logits).

items.question: How many learnable parameters are in a fully connected layer that maps 128 inputs to 64 neurons (including biases)?
items.answer: 128 * 64 weights + 64 biases = 8192 + 64 = 8256 parameters. Each of the 64 neurons has 128 input weights plus one bias.

items.question: In an interview you are asked why we implement a layer of neurons as one matrix multiply instead of looping over neurons. What is the concise answer?
items.answer: All neurons in a layer apply different weight vectors to the same input activation vector. Packing those weight vectors as rows (or columns, depending on convention) of W turns the layer into X @ W + b, which processes an entire mini-batch in one call and maps to highly optimized linear algebra on CPU or GPU.

items.question: A colleague says their model has depth 5 because it lists five nn.Module entries: Linear, ReLU, Linear, ReLU, Linear. How do you correct them?
items.answer: Depth counts hidden transformation layers (Linear modules between input and output), not activation functions. That stack has two hidden Linear layers (depth 2) plus one output Linear — ReLU modules are nonlinearities applied after a layer, not additional hidden tiers.
